narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 30, and rankings in the 4th decile for Year 1 grades and 1st decile for Year 3 at your school, along with your plan to attend part-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from thousands of past students, estimated a 56% chance that you would fail the bar exam—tipping the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 academic decile, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, especially since it was much lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile ranking. While your Year 1 decile was somewhat below average, it actually worked slightly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score, both below the dataset averages of 3.21 and 36.7 respectively, provided modest positive influences toward passing, showing some resilience in your record. Planning to attend part-time, which is less common than the full-time path chosen by over 90% of students, also gently supported a passing prediction in the model's eyes, as did your family income bracket. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having strengths in personal circumstances but notable gaps in later academic performance that outweighed them here. I know this rejection stings, especially when you've come this far, but it's not a judgment on your potential—it's one model's data-driven call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring full-time options if feasible. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying stronger. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for tailored strategies. You've got this.
